title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a gathering, Joyce claims she witnessed a murder when she was very young, though others like Miss Whitaker, Mrs. Dreck, Anne, and Cassie dismiss her story as nonsense. Joyce explains she did not contact the police because she only realized the event was a murder months ago after hearing someone else mention it. When Beatrice asks for details regarding the victim and the killer, Joyce refuses to answer because she finds Beatrice annoying.

As the group prepares for an apple-biting game, a debate ensues regarding whether a galvanized pail or a plastic bucket is better; they conclude the metal pail is superior despite the plastic bucket being more attractive. Mrs. Oliver, feeling guilty after being teased about her love for apples, seeks privacy in the "Apple Orchard" house. On her way, she encounters a young boy and girl, approximately fifteen and twelve years old, embracing against a door. After Mrs. Oliver asks to pass, the lovers reluctantly separate and angrily call her selfish. Mrs. Oliver enters the house and locks the door behind her.

Later, Ariadne Oliver discusses the complexities of parties with Judith Butler. Judith suggests that Roya Drake is an excellent organizer. At 7:30 PM, the party begins, featuring decorations of red, blue, and yellow pumpkins. Roya Drake announces a program including a broomstick competition, cutting cornbread in a greenhouse, an apple-biting contest, dancing, a mirror ritual in the small study, dinner, and a dragon hunt. During the broomstick competition, Roya explains her motive for using small brooms is to ensure prizes are distributed more evenly among the children. The festivities continue with the apple-biting game and a performance by Mrs. Goodbody, a local cleaning woman playing a witch. The witch performs a ritual for Beatrice, instructing her to hold a mirror and wait for the lights to go out to see the face of her future husband.
